items.title: Medical test for a rare disease
items.scenario: A disease affects 0.1% of the population. A test is 99% sensitive (P(positive|disease)) and 95% specific (P(negative|healthy)). A patient tests positive. How likely are they to actually have the disease?
items.explanation: Let D = disease, + = positive test. P(D) = 0.001, P(+|D) = 0.99, P(+|Dᶜ) = 0.05. P(+) = 0.99×0.001 + 0.05×0.999 ≈ 0.0509. Bayes gives P(D|+) = 0.99×0.001 / 0.0509 ≈ 0.019—only about 1.9%. The rare prior dominates despite a good test.

items.title: Spam filter false-alarm rate
items.scenario: 1% of inbox mail is spam. The filter catches 90% of spam but also flags 5% of legitimate mail. An email lands in the spam folder—what is P(actually spam | flagged)?
items.explanation: P(S) = 0.01, P(F|S) = 0.90, P(F|ham) = 0.05. P(F) ≈ 0.0585. P(S|F) = 0.90×0.01 / 0.0585 ≈ 0.154. Most flagged mail is still ham because ham is abundant.

items.title: Drawing a card after seeing a face card
items.scenario: From a standard 52-card deck, you are told the drawn card is a face card (J, Q, or K). What is P(the card is a King | face card)?
items.explanation: P(K) = 4/52, P(face) = 12/52, and every King is a face card so P(face|K) = 1. Bayes: P(K|face) = P(face|K)P(K)/P(face) = 1 × (4/52) / (12/52) = 4/12 = 1/3.

items.title: LLM safety filter triage
items.scenario: A content moderation model flags 2% of prompts as policy violations. Among truly violating prompts it catches 85%; among safe prompts it false-flags 1%. A flagged prompt arrives—what is P(actually violating | flagged)?
items.explanation: P(V) = 0.02, P(F|V) = 0.85, P(F|safe) = 0.01. P(F) = 0.85×0.02 + 0.01×0.98 = 0.027. P(V|F) = 0.85×0.02 / 0.027 ≈ 0.63. Human review queues should expect a substantial false-flag rate even with a strong classifier.
